context: Current closing price of Apple (AAPL): $259.20 USD as of 2026-04-13.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a broadly stable but gently upward-drifting baseline. For the one-day horizon, I anchored close to the most recent observed price or level, assuming mean-reversion dominates over very short windows and that no major catalyst is imminent. The one-week estimate nudges slightly higher, reflecting a modest positive drift assumption consistent with recent trend momentum, while acknowledging that short-term noise tends to average out. The one-month estimate extends that drift further, embedding a slightly more optimistic bias based on the assumption that the underlying fundamental or macro backdrop remains constructive, though the increment is deliberately modest because compounding uncertainty makes confident directional calls at that horizon difficult.

The confidence interval widths were calibrated primarily to realized volatility at each horizon. For one day, recent daily price ranges suggested a relatively tight band, giving a roughly ±5–7% corridor at the 90% level. Over one week, volatility compounds and the range of plausible macro or news-driven surprises widens considerably, so I expanded the interval to roughly ±10%. At one month, the interval reflects not just compounded daily volatility but also the genuine possibility of regime shifts — a new macro narrative, a policy surprise, or a sector rotation — which pushed the band to approximately ±17–18% around the point estimate.

The principal tail risks that could push outcomes outside my stated intervals include sudden shifts in central bank policy communication, a geopolitical shock, or an unexpected corporate or economic data release that reprices the entire risk environment. On the upside, a stronger-than-expected economic print or a resolution to an ongoing geopolitical tension could trigger a sharp rally beyond the upper bound. On the downside, a credit event, a hawkish policy surprise, or a liquidity squeeze — particularly one amplified by thin market conditions — could drive prices well below the lower confidence bound.

It is worth noting that my intervals are inherently model-dependent and assume a rough continuity of the current volatility regime. If realized volatility spikes or correlations break down — as they tend to during stress periods — even the 90% intervals should be treated as optimistic. Black swan events by definition fall outside any probabilistic framework calibrated to normal conditions, so these forecasts should be interpreted as conditional on the absence of truly extraordinary disruptions.
